CREATE TABLE ip_addresses (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, ip inet NOT NULL);
SELECT * FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.2';
SELECT ip FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.1' AND id = 1;
SELECT id FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.4' ORDER BY id DESC;
SELECT a.ip FROM ip_addresses a JOIN ip_addresses b ON a.ip <> b.ip WHERE a.id < b.id;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.1' AND ip <> inet '192.168.1.2';
CREATE TABLE user_data (user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, user_ip inet NOT NULL);
SELECT user_id FROM user_data WHERE user_ip <> (SELECT ip FROM ip_addresses WHERE id = 1);
SELECT a.user_id FROM user_data a LEFT JOIN ip_addresses b ON a.user_ip <> b.ip WHERE b.ip IS NULL;
SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.5') AS not_equal_check;
WITH different_ips AS (SELECT ip FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> inet '192.168.1.2') SELECT COUNT(*) FROM different_ips;
CREATE TABLE settings (setting_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, setting_value inet NOT NULL);
SELECT * FROM settings WHERE setting_value <> (SELECT setting_value FROM settings WHERE setting_id = 1);
SELECT DISTINCT ip FROM ip_addresses WHERE ip <> ANY (SELECT setting_value FROM settings);
